title: Precision@K and Recall@K
description:
Implement Precision@K and Recall@K for evaluating a retrieval system.

Given a ranked list of retrieved document IDs and a set of relevant document IDs:

- **Precision@K** = |relevant docs in top-K retrieved| / K
- **Recall@K** = |relevant docs in top-K retrieved| / |total relevant docs|

**Example — step-by-step computation:**

```
retrieved = ["d1", "d3", "d5", "d2", "d8"]   # ranked list from retrieval system
relevant  = {"d1", "d2", "d5", "d9"}            # ground-truth relevant docs
K = 3

Top-3 retrieved: ["d1", "d3", "d5"]

Which of these are relevant?
  "d1" -> YES (in relevant set)
  "d3" -> NO
  "d5" -> YES (in relevant set)

|relevant in top-3| = 2

Precision@3 = 2 / 3 = 0.6667
Recall@3    = 2 / 4 = 0.5000   (4 = total number of relevant docs)
```

Implement both functions below.
constraints: If K > len(retrieved), cap K at len(retrieved)
If there are no relevant documents (empty relevant set), Recall@K should return 0.0
starter_code:
def precision_at_k(retrieved: list, relevant: set, k: int) -> float:
    """Compute Precision@K.

    Args:
        retrieved: List of document IDs in ranked order
        relevant: Set of relevant document IDs
        k: Cutoff position

    Returns:
        Precision@K as a float
    """
    pass


def recall_at_k(retrieved: list, relevant: set, k: int) -> float:
    """Compute Recall@K.

    Args:
        retrieved: List of document IDs in ranked order
        relevant: Set of relevant document IDs
        k: Cutoff position

    Returns:
        Recall@K as a float
    """
    pass
explanation:
Precision@K measures what fraction of the top-K retrieved documents are actually relevant. It penalizes retrieving irrelevant documents. Recall@K measures what fraction of all relevant documents appear in the top-K results. It penalizes missing relevant documents.

These two metrics capture a fundamental trade-off: increasing K tends to improve recall (you retrieve more relevant docs) but can hurt precision (you also retrieve more irrelevant docs). Together they give a balanced view of retrieval quality.

The implementation is straightforward: slice the retrieved list to the top K items, count how many are in the relevant set, then divide by K (for precision) or by the total number of relevant documents (for recall).

test_cases.name: Basic precision and recall
test_cases.code:
retrieved = ['d1', 'd3', 'd5', 'd2', 'd8']
relevant = {'d1', 'd2', 'd5', 'd9'}
assert abs(precision_at_k(retrieved, relevant, 3) - 2/3) < 1e-9
assert abs(recall_at_k(retrieved, relevant, 3) - 0.5) < 1e-9

test_cases.name: Perfect precision at K=1
test_cases.code:
retrieved = ['d1', 'd3', 'd5']
relevant = {'d1', 'd2'}
assert abs(precision_at_k(retrieved, relevant, 1) - 1.0) < 1e-9
assert abs(recall_at_k(retrieved, relevant, 1) - 0.5) < 1e-9

test_cases.name: No relevant docs in top-K
test_cases.code:
retrieved = ['d3', 'd4', 'd6']
relevant = {'d1', 'd2'}
assert precision_at_k(retrieved, relevant, 3) == 0.0
assert recall_at_k(retrieved, relevant, 3) == 0.0

test_cases.name: K larger than retrieved list
test_cases.code:
retrieved = ['d1', 'd2']
relevant = {'d1', 'd2', 'd3'}
assert abs(precision_at_k(retrieved, relevant, 10) - 1.0) < 1e-9
assert abs(recall_at_k(retrieved, relevant, 10) - 2/3) < 1e-9

test_cases.name: Empty relevant set returns 0 recall
test_cases.code:
retrieved = ['d1', 'd2', 'd3']
relevant = set()
assert recall_at_k(retrieved, relevant, 2) == 0.0

test_cases.name: All retrieved are relevant (perfect)
test_cases.code:
retrieved = ['d1', 'd2', 'd3']
relevant = {'d1', 'd2', 'd3'}
assert abs(precision_at_k(retrieved, relevant, 3) - 1.0) < 1e-9
assert abs(recall_at_k(retrieved, relevant, 3) - 1.0) < 1e-9

hints: Slice the retrieved list to get only the first K items, capping K at the list length.
Use a set membership check to count how many of the top-K documents are relevant.
Be careful with the denominator: Precision divides by K, Recall divides by total relevant docs.
